narration.default: Last tutorial your function did the SAME thing every time you called it — print Hello! Today you'll write functions that take INFORMATION when called, and behave differently depending on what they get. Same command name, many different answers. The information is called a parameter.

steps.title: Add and subtract — two functions, two parameters each

narration.default:
Type:

def multiply(a, b):
    print(a * b)

Three functions now, all defined but none called yet. Each one takes two numbers, does some maths with them, and prints the answer. Notice how similar the three definitions look — same shape, just a different operator. That's a hint we'll improve later.

Below the defs (no indent), type:

add(5, 3)
subtract(10, 4)
multiply(2, 6)

When you call add(5, 3), the parameter a gets 5 and b gets 3 — like Python's filling the empty boxes. The function runs, prints 8, and finishes. Each call below uses different numbers — the same function gives a different answer each time.

Click Run. The terminal prints 8, 6, 12 — the answers from each call. Same trio of functions could now be reused with any numbers. Try add(100, 200) or multiply(7, 7) — the function definitions never change, only what you pass in.

Two words you'll hear: PARAMETER is the empty box in the function definition (a and b). ARGUMENT is the actual value you pass in when you call (5 and 3). Same idea, just the names differ depending on which side you're on. Don't worry about getting the names right — most programmers use them interchangeably.

Right now your functions PRINT but don't give back a value you can use. So you can't write x = add(5, 3) and then USE x. To make that work, the function needs to hand the value back instead of just printing it. Python has a keyword for exactly that — return. Once a function returns its answer, the caller can store it, print it, or feed it into another function — calculate a price with tax, look up a player's score from their name, build a chain of small calculators each handing the answer to the next.
